questions.specification:
## Economic Dynamics in Angola

Angola, located in southwestern Africa, is one of the continent’s largest oil producers, with over 90% of its exports tied to crude oil. In recent years, its real GDP growth has been volatile, fluctuating between –1.2% and 2.4% annually from 2018 to 2022, reflecting significant dependence on global energy prices. Despite this oil dominance, the Angolan government has embarked on diversification initiatives to stabilize the economy and reduce vulnerability to external shocks.

Inflationary pressures have been a persistent challenge. In 2021, annual inflation reached 22%, eroding household purchasing power, particularly among lower-income groups. The Banco Nacional de Angola has responded with tight monetary policy, raising benchmark interest rates to 20% in 2022 to curb excess liquidity. Meanwhile, high food costs and depreciation of the kwanza (Angola’s currency) continue to push up import prices.

Angola’s external debt stood at approximately US$52 billion by the end of 2022, prompting government measures to manage debt-service obligations. The nation has engaged in negotiations with international organizations to secure favorable terms. While these arrangements have enabled the financing of infrastructure projects, critics argue that the burden of debt repayment poses risks to future fiscal stability.

In the domestic arena, the government introduced targeted subsidies for agricultural inputs such as fertilizer and seeds to incentivize local food production. However, some policymakers and economists question the efficiency of these subsidies, citing the risk of resource misallocation and corruption. At the same time, partial privatization of several state-owned enterprises aims to encourage private sector investment and improve overall productivity.

Beyond the oil sector, Angola’s economy relies on diamonds, fisheries, and agriculture. In 2022, Angola exported approximately US$1.3 billion worth of diamonds, representing a crucial revenue source after oil. To further diversify, the government has prioritized investments in education and health to strengthen human capital, hoping to spur growth in technology-driven and service-based industries. Infrastructure expansion, especially in rural areas, is underway to bridge the urban-rural divide and stimulate market integration.

International trade relationships are also undergoing transformation. While Angola remains an active member of the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), it has recently signed trade facilitation agreements to reduce import tariffs on selected capital goods. This move aims to attract foreign direct investment (FDI) in manufacturing, but concerns persist about local firms’ competitiveness, given power supply challenges and limited access to finance. Angola’s central bank manages the exchange rate of the kwanza, occasionally intervening in currency markets to stabilize fluctuations arising from volatile oil earnings.

Socioeconomic disparities are still evident, with an estimated Gini coefficient of 0.54 in 2021. Although the government has expanded health and education programs, rural communities remain underserved. Youth unemployment, recorded at 28% in 2022, underscores the need for better job creation policies and entrepreneurial support. The success of Angola’s economic reforms will likely hinge on sustained political commitment, effective resource management, and the gradual diversification away from oil-dependence toward a resilient, broad-based economy.

**Table 1: Angola’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2022)**

| Indicator                     |  2018    |  2019    |  2020    |  2021    |  2022    |
|------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)    | 105      | 100      |   95     |   98     |  103     |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)     | –1.2     |  0.5     | –4.0     |  0.7     |  2.4     |
| Inflation Rate (%)           | 17.5     | 16.9     | 25.1     | 22.0     | 18.5     |
| Budget Balance (% of GDP)    | –4.2     | –3.6     | –2.0     | –3.2     | –2.7     |
| Exchange Rate (Kwanza/US$)   | 250      | 310      |  480     |  520     |  500     |

**Table 2: Private Sector and Export Data for Angola (2018–2022)**

| Indicator                                    | 2018  | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|----------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                    |  3.2  |  2.4  |  1.8  |  2.1  |  2.9  |
| Private Sector Contribution to GDP (%)       | 40.5  | 41.0  | 41.3  | 42.1  | 43.5  |
| Diamond Exports (US$ billion)               |  1.1  |  1.2  |  1.0  |  1.1  |  1.3  |
| Non-Oil Exports (US$ billion)               |  2.5  |  3.0  |  2.7  |  3.1  |  3.5  |
| Agriculture Subsidies (US$ million)          |  210  |  250  |  260  |  280  |  305  |

parts.content: Define the term monetary policy indicated in the text (paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: Monetary policy refers to the adjustments of the interest rates and the availability of money in the economy set by the central bank.

parts.content: List two ways in which partial privatization of state-owned enterprises (paragraph 4) might promote efficiency in Angola’s economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Improved managerial incentives, as private stakeholders seek greater efficiency and profitability.  
- Enhanced accountability, as private sector involvement may reduce inefficiencies typical of state-owned entities.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the absolute change (in US$ billions) in Angola’s nominal GDP between 2018 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
Step-by-step calculation:  
- 2022 Nominal GDP – 2018 Nominal GDP = 103 – 105 = –2 (US$ billion)  
- The absolute change is –2 (a decrease of US$2 billion).

parts.content: Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how infrastructure investments (paragraph 5) could affect Angola’s long-run productive capacity.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/df58f0aa-7eb5-41e0-ae27-7c68f23dc460)

- A well-developed infrastructure reduces transport costs, enhances productivity, and attracts more investment.  
- Improved infrastructure shifts the PPC outward(from PPC1 to PPC2), representing an increase in the maximum potential output of both capital-intensive and labor-intensive goods.  
- As rural areas become more integrated, previously underutilized resources may be brought into production, further raising potential GDP.  
- In a PPC diagram, this is shown by an outward shift, indicating higher potential output of all goods.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how increased trade facilitation agreements (paragraph 6) might impact the exchange rate of the kwanza.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f6ed4cac-bb2e-400e-8c71-9b77be88cb0a)

- Reduced import tariffs on capital goods can lower production costs, enhance export competitiveness, and boost foreign investors’ interest in local production.  
- Greater demand for Angolan goods or capital inflows increases demand for the kwanza, potentially causing an appreciation of the currency.  
- On a demand-supply-of-currency diagram, the demand curve for kwanza shifts right(from D1 to D2) as foreign buyers increase purchases of kwanza to invest or buy exports, raising the exchange rate(from e1 to e2) if supply remains constant.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a market failure diagram (negative externalities or subsidies), explain one potential inefficiency arising from Angola’s agricultural subsidies (paragraph 4).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1049754b-b706-435d-971a-b3dd25e42e27)

- Subsidies lower farmers’ production costs, boosting output beyond the socially optimal level (Qe<Qsub) if the subsidy is not well-targeted.  
- This can distort price signals, funneling resources into less productive uses or encouraging corruption/overuse of inputs.  
- In a subsidy diagram, the supply curve shifts to the right, (S -> S+sub) leading to overproduction and a potential welfare loss. The market output exceeds the level where marginal social cost equals marginal social benefit.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain the link between Angola’s GDP growth volatility (Table 1) and cyclical unemployment (paragraph 7).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/648b1928-de14-4190-854a-a390712229ac)

- Negative growth years (–1.2% in 2018, –4.0% in 2020) likely raise unemployment, as firms reduce production and labor demand.  
- Positive growth (2.4% in 2022) can lower unemployment by increasing aggregate demand for goods and services.  
- In a business cycle diagram, Angola’s economy moves from recessionary (negative or low GDP growth) to expansionary (positive GDP growth), causing cyclical unemployment to rise in recessions and fall in expansions.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, discuss the impact of rising private sector participation (Table 2) on Angola’s long-term economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Economic Growth: An increase in the real output of goods and services in an economy over time.  
- Economic Development: A broader concept involving improvements in living standards, access to healthcare, education, and an equitable income distribution.  
- Private Sector Participation: The increasing role of private firms and investment in the production of goods and services.

**Range of Arguments:**

1. Potential for Higher Efficiency and Innovation  
	- Greater private sector participation (Table 2 shows rising FDI inflows from US 1.8 billion in 2020 to US 2.9 billion in 2022) encourages competition and innovation, stimulating faster growth.  
	- Profit motives lead private firms to seek cost-effective practices, adopt new technologies, and engage in research and development.  
	- Improved efficiency can increase total output, thereby raising GDP and contributing to development through job creation and higher incomes.
	- Adapting new technologies will increase Angola's economic capacity and increase potential output, causing a rightward shift of LRAS, from LRAS1 to LRAS2. 
	
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ddeedebe-1720-4a84-9f1d-88a20cc05f8c)

2. Diversification Away from Oil Dependency  
	- As the private sector’s contribution to GDP rises (from 40.5% in 2018 to 43.5% in 2022), reliance on oil as the primary source of income may gradually decrease.  
	- Potential growth in manufacturing and services reduces vulnerability to external shocks in global energy markets.  
	- A more diverse economy tends to exhibit more stable growth, which supports long-term development through consistent government revenues for social spending.

3. Employment Generation and Human Capital Development  
	- Private investment in industries such as agriculture, diamonds, fisheries, and potentially manufacturing can generate new jobs, helping to address youth unemployment of 28% (paragraph 7).  
	- Growth in the private sector can spur more investment in education and training, fostering human capital development, crucial for long-term growth and social progress.  
	- 
	- Higher employment raises incomes, contributing to improved standards of living.

4. Government Revenue and Public Investment  
	- Successful private enterprises can expand the tax base if properly regulated. Government revenue from taxes can finance public goods (education, healthcare, infrastructure).  
	- With enhanced revenue, the government can potentially reduce the burden of external debt and allocate funds to diversify economic activities.  
	- Crowding-in effects might occur if government infrastructure spending complements private initiatives rather than competing with them.

5. Constraints and Limitations  
	- Power supply challenges and limited access to finance may restrict the scope of private sector expansion, as stated in paragraph 6.  
	- If regulatory frameworks are weak, increased privatization might lead to market dominance by a few powerful firms, reducing overall efficiency.
	- Socioeconomic disparities (Gini coefficient at 0.54) might persist if private sector gains are concentrated among higher-income groups.

6. Balance of Payments and External Stability  
	- Rising private sector exports evidenced by increasing non-oil exports (from US 2.5 billion in 2018 to US 3.5 billion in 2022) could improve the current account balance over time.  
	- FDI inflows can finance sustainable projects, but there is also a risk of profit repatriation and capital flight if global economic conditions worsen.

**Conclusion:**

- Although private sector growth can foster efficiency, diversification, and employment, careful government oversight is crucial to avoid income inequality and ensure that rising output translates into broad-based improvements in welfare.  
- Sound infrastructure, stable macroeconomic policies, and transparent governance further enhance the positive impact of private sector participation on long-term economic growth and development.

questions.specification:
Serbia is a country located in Southeastern Europe with a population of approximately 6.9 million. Its official currency is the Serbian dinar (RSD). According to official estimates, Serbia’s nominal GDP in 2022 reached US\$65.2 billion, growing from US$63.5 billion in 2021. The unemployment rate stands at 9.8%, although it can be higher in rural areas. Inflation in 2022 averaged about 11.5%, driven partly by increasing energy prices.

Serbia is well-known for its agricultural output—especially raspberries, where it ranks among the top producers in the world. Domestically, the government charges a 20% value added tax (VAT) on many goods and services. The personal income tax system has a base rate of 10%, rising gradually for higher income brackets, while corporate income is taxed at 15%. The Gini coefficient stands at 0.35, suggesting moderate income inequality.

Trade is a constant focus of Serbian economic policy. Serbia has strong trade links with the European Union (EU), which accounts for roughly 63% of its exports. Policymakers have debated using expansionary fiscal policy to support economic growth, focusing on infrastructure development and public investment.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Serbia (2022)  
| Indicator               | Value          |
|-------------------------|----------------|
| Population             | 6.9 million    |
| Nominal GDP (US$)      | 65.2 billion   |
| GDP in 2021 (US$)      | 63.5 billion   |
| Unemployment rate      | 9.8%           |
| Inflation rate         | 11.5%          |
| Gini coefficient       | 0.35           |

Table 2: Labour Market Data for Serbia (2022)  
| Population Over 15 (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 5.0                            | 3.5                  | 0.38                   |

Table 3: Domestic Market for Raspberries in Serbia  
| Price (RSD per kg) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (tonnes) |
|--------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| 300                | 680,000                   | 640,000                    |
| 350                | 620,000                   | 680,000                    |

Figure 1 (not shown) illustrates the domestic demand (Dd) and supply (Sd) for raspberries in Serbia, where the equilibrium price in 2022 was 300 RSD per kg. By early 2023, the price reached 350 RSD per kg as global demand picked up.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the unemployment rate in Serbia for 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Sum of labour force = 3.5 million (employed) + 0.38 million (unemployed) = 3.88 million.  
- Unemployment rate = (Unemployed ÷ Labour force) × 100 = (0.38 ÷ 3.88) × 100 ≈ 9.8%.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate from 2021 to 2022 for Serbia.
parts.markscheme:
- Nominal GDP in 2021: US$63.5 billion; Nominal GDP in 2022: USD 65.2 billion.  
- Nominal GDP growth rate ≈ [(65.2 − 63.5) ÷ 63.5] × 100 ≈ 2.68%.  
- Approximate real GDP growth rate ≈ 2.68% - 11.5% (inflation) ≈ −8.82%.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3 (and Figure 1), calculate the price elasticity of demand for raspberries when the price increases from 300 RSD to 350 RSD.
Using information from Table 3 (and Figure 1), calculate the price elasticity of supply for raspberries when the price increases from 300 RSD to 350 RSD.
Define the term “Keynesian multiplier.”
parts.markscheme: The Keynesian multiplier refers to the process by which an initial injection into the economy leads to a proportionately larger change in national income.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government infrastructure spending might affect Serbia’s real output.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/88adff2c-c0cd-413a-aded-f7c3f161530f)

- Increasing government spending shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right (AD1 -> AD2), raising the equilibrium level of real output from Y to Yp.  
- This can also push up the price level (pl1 -> pl2) if the economy is near full capacity.  
- If spare capacity exists (Real GDP (Y) is lower than Yp), real output can rise significantly with limited inflationary pressure.  
- The overall impact depends on the shape of the AS curve and the size of the multiplier effect.  

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate Serbia’s approximate post-tax Gini coefficient if new government policy successfully reduces income inequality by 14%.
parts.markscheme:
- Initial Gini coefficient = 0.35
- New Gini coefficient ≈ 0.35 − (0.14 × 0.35) = 0.35 − 0.049 = 0.301

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain how continuing inflation at 11.5% might impact real wages in Serbia.
parts.markscheme:
- Prolonged inflation at 11.5% tends to erode the purchasing power of wages if nominal wages do not increase at the same rate.  
- Workers experience a reduction in real income and can afford fewer goods and services.  
- Firms may face pressure to raise wages, which could increase production costs and potentially feed into further price increases.  
- High inflation can lead to uncertainty and reduced consumer confidence, unless wages keep pace with price changes.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Serbia in order to reduce the unemployment rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Unemployment refers to the people in the labour force who are actively seeking work but remain jobless, and an unemployment rate of 9.8% indicates a significant portion of the population without jobs.  
- Expansionary fiscal policy can shift aggregate demand outward, potentially creating new employment opportunities; this aligns with the text mentioning infrastructure development and public investment.  
- Increased government spending on roads, public transport, and other infrastructure projects can employ more workers directly and stimulate private sector activity, especially in rural areas with higher unemployment.  
- The Keynesian multiplier suggests that an initial injection of government spending can lead to a larger increase in overall income, boosting consumption and further reducing unemployment through higher aggregate demand.  
- Stronger public investment may attract additional foreign direct investment (FDI) if improved infrastructure raises productivity, which can create complementary private-sector jobs and support international trade links.  
- Robust infrastructure can benefit the agricultural sector, including raspberries, by reducing transportation costs and enhancing competitiveness, thereby sustaining jobs in rural regions.  
- The diagram displays a rightward shift of the aggregate demand curve from AD1 to AD2, driven by increased government spending on infrastructure. This shift raises real GDP (Y -> Yp), increases price level (pl1 -> pl2) and lowers unemployment as higher demand for goods and services boosts production and job creation. The multiplier effect amplifies this impact, while idle capacity in the economy allows for non-inflationary growth—highlighting how fiscal stimulus can address Serbia’s 9.8% unemployment rate.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/88adff2c-c0cd-413a-aded-f7c3f161530f)

- A risk involves higher government debt or potential inflationary pressures if the economy nears full capacity; however, Serbia’s current GDP figures and existing unemployment suggest room for output expansion.  
- Improving workforce skills alongside infrastructure spending through targeted training programs can reduce structural unemployment in sectors that demand new skills, helping long-term employment stability.  
- Balancing the potential budget deficit against the social advantages of lower unemployment may require complementary measures, such as slight adjustments in tax structures or careful prioritization of projects.  
- This policy targets unemployment directly, supports growth in key industries highlighted in the case study, and may lead to a more equitable distribution of income if sustained over time.

questions.specification:
## Recent Economic Trends in Chile

Chile, located along the western coast of South America, is widely regarded as one of the region’s most stable and prosperous nations. With a population of around 19 million, the country boasts a successful track record in macroeconomic management, marked by consistent economic growth and relatively low government debt levels. However, ongoing shifts in global trade, fluctuating copper prices, and recent policy reforms have brought new challenges to Chile’s economy.

In 2022, Chile recorded an average monthly wage of approximately US$600, though the cost of living in major urban centers such as Santiago continues to rise. To maintain price stability, the Central Bank of Chile has long operated an inflation-targeting regime, typically aiming for annual inflation close to 3%. Yet external pressures—like disruptions to global supply chains—pushed the inflation rate up to 7.2% in 2022. Unemployment remains a pressing issue; following a peak of 10.7% in 2020 when economic activity contracted, joblessness has gradually declined as the economy recovers.

Chile’s economic identity is strongly tied to mining, particularly copper, which accounts for a significant proportion of export revenues. In 2022, approximately 45% of total exports came from copper and other minerals. While copper has been a major driver of economic growth, economists and policymakers increasingly emphasize diversification to protect against commodity price volatility. The government has also expanded support for agricultural and service industries, promoting increased global competitiveness through various trade agreements with North American and Asian partners.

On the fiscal side, Chile historically prided itself on low government debt, yet debt levels have slowly risen to 37% of GDP by 2022. This reflects higher spending on social programs, including public healthcare and education subsidies. Policymakers are attempting to strike a balance between prudent fiscal management and ensuring equitable access to basic services. In the microeconomic arena, Chile introduced an excise tax on sugar-sweetened beverages to discourage unhealthy consumption and reduce negative externalities tied to rising obesity rates.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) flows remain relatively stable in non-mining ventures, particularly in renewable energy sectors such as solar and wind. The government has enacted regulatory changes that encourage private-sector participation in green investments, hoping to lessen reliance on fossil fuels. Analysts predict that over the next decade, renewable energy might comprise up to 30% of Chile’s energy mix, helping the country manage environmental externalities while sustaining long-term economic growth.

Despite Chile’s liberalized trade regime, some domestic industries face competitiveness hurdles from global market fluctuations. The peso’s exchange rate is influenced by copper prices. Therefore, this has spurred officials to pursue greater diversification.

Income distribution remains a topic of debate. Chile has recorded improvements in its Gini coefficient over the past decade, yet inequalities persist—especially in rural areas where access to education and healthcare lags behind that in urban regions. Government initiatives to raise the minimum wage and invest in vocational training signal attempts to address income disparities, which some critics argue need more comprehensive policies.

Private enterprise plays a central role in Chile’s leading export industries. In the mining sector, large multinational firms partner with domestic companies, creating jobs and contributing to government revenue. Nevertheless, critics point to environmental costs from mining activities and the need for stricter regulations to ensure sustainable resource use. Many also question whether enough investments are being channeled into non-traditional sectors like technology and advanced manufacturing—areas widely seen as key to sustainable future growth.

Moving forward, Chile’s policy landscape continues to evolve. Discussions about strengthening social safety nets, investing further in green energy, and maintaining a competitive exchange rate occupy center stage. The government’s approach to promoting inclusive development includes balancing social spending with structural reforms that attract both domestic and foreign investors. Ultimately, Chile’s ability to diversify its economy beyond copper and ensure equity across various regions will determine its long-term path to stable and inclusive growth.

**Table 1: Chile’s Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                      | 2019 | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|--------------------------------|------|-------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP Growth (%)            | 1.1  | –5.8  | 11.7  | 2.3   |
| Inflation Rate (%)             | 2.2  | 3.0   | 4.5   | 7.2   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)          | 7.0  | 10.7  | 8.9   | 7.5   |
| Exchange Rate (CLP per US$)    | 698  | 793   | 725   | 785   |
| Government Debt (% of GDP)     | 28   | 33    | 35    | 37    |

Because of the higher price, consumers reduce their consumption of sugary drinks, contributing to a decline in negative externalities such as health costs related to obesity. 
- In a demand and supply diagram, the supply curve shifts vertically upward, causing equilibrium quantity to fall and consumer price to rise.

This tends to lower cyclical unemployment because, as real GDP grows, firms increase production and hire additional workers, reducing joblessness caused by a lack of overall demand in the economy.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Chile’s private mining sector on the country’s long-term economic growth and development prospects.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 

**Definiton:**

- **Long-term economic growth**: Sustained increase in a country’s productive capacity over time.  
- **Economic development**: Improvements in standards of living, reduction in poverty, and enhanced social welfare, typically measured by indicators such as literacy, health, and income distribution. 

**Range of Arguments and Evaluation:**

- Contribution to GDP and export revenues  
  - Copper mining generates about 45% of Chile’s total exports (Paragraph 3), supplying valuable foreign exchange.  
  - Higher export earnings may boost real GDP, stimulating aggregate demand and promoting investment in supporting infrastructure.  
  - Evaluation: Overreliance on copper leaves Chile vulnerable to global commodity price swings, risking instability in growth if copper prices plunge.  

- Foreign direct investment and technology transfer  
  - The sector attracts FDI, which can enhance technology transfer and productivity, contributing to long-term growth.  
  - FDI also stimulates other industries transportation, services, and engineering fostering diversification if managed effectively.  
  - Evaluation: FDI may focus narrowly on extractive industries without significantly diversifying the economy or building human capital outside of mining
  - Increase in both FDI and copper exports results in a righward shift in Agregate Demand from AD1 to AD2.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5719680d-ba11-45f0-bd20-c67ec9713e77).  

- Employment and income generation  
  - Multinational mining firms partner with local companies, creating jobs in the mining supply chain (Paragraph 8).  
  - Job creation can increase household incomes and support broader economic development, especially in mining-intensive regions.  
  - Evaluation: The economic benefits may be uneven if jobs are concentrated regionally or if wages for lower-skilled workers in mining are insufficient relative to rising living costs.  



- Government revenue and social programs  
  - Mining royalties and taxes fund social services like health care and education (Paragraph 4).  
  - These revenues help maintain relatively low government debt and support social welfare, promoting development.  
  - Evaluation: Rising social spending has pushed government debt to 37% of GDP, so overdependence on mining revenues could challenge fiscal sustainability if commodity prices fall.  

- Environmental consequences and sustainability  
  - Mining operations can create negative environmental externalities, such as pollution and water overuse (Paragraph 8).  
  - Strict regulations and investments in greener techniques can reduce environmental harm and maintain longer-term resource viability.  
  - Evaluation: Environmental degradation may undermine sustainable development, resulting in external costs that hinder social welfare if not addressed effectively from negative production externalities.  

- Potential for diversification and long-term stability  
  - Emphasis on developing service and agricultural sectors aims to broaden Chile’s export base (Paragraph 3).  
  - Reducing overdependence on commodities could smooth economic shocks and sustain growth.  
  - Evaluation: Genuine diversification requires structural reforms, improved infrastructure, and skill development. Mining profits alone might not be sufficient if they are not reinvested in emerging industries.  

**Conclusion:**

- The private mining sector has propelled Chile’s economic growth by generating export revenue, employment, and government income for social spending through resource-driven expansion. 
- However, its impact on sustainable development rests on mitigating volatility, addressing environmental concerns, and channeling resources toward diversification. 
- Balancing mining revenues with balanced regional development, social investments, and greener policies is crucial for long-term growth and broader societal welfare.

parts.content: Explain how the central bank can change the minimum reserve requirements to decrease interest rates.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition:

- **Central Bank**: The authority responsible for formulating and implementing monetary policy, regulating the banking system, and controlling the money supply.

- **Minimum Reserve Requirements**: The proportion of customer deposits that commercial banks must hold in reserve and cannot lend out.

- **Interest Rates**: The cost of borrowing or the reward for saving, typically expressed as a percentage of the amount borrowed or saved.

### Economic Theory:

- Commercial banks are required to hold a certain percentage of their deposits as reserves, limiting the amount they can lend out to consumers and businesses.

- Then, when the central bank lowers the minimum reserve requirements, banks are able to lend a higher proportion of their deposits, effectively increasing the supply of loanable funds in the economy.

- Consequently, an increased supply of loanable funds results in a rightward shift of the money supply curve in the money market (from Sm1 to Sm2), leading to a lower equilibrium interest rate (i1 to i2).

- Also, lower interest rates make borrowing more attractive for consumers and firms, stimulating consumption and investment. This can help boost aggregate demand, encouraging economic activity.

- Therefore, if the central bank believes that the economy requires stimulus, reducing the minimum reserve requirements is one policy tool that can be employed to achieve lower interest rates and increase liquidity in the financial system.

### Diagram (Money Market):

- Depicts the initial supply of money (Sm1) intersecting with money demand (D) at interest rate i1.

- A decrease in reserve requirements shifts the supply curve to the right (Sm2), resulting in a new, lower interest rate i2 and higher quantity of money in circulation (Q2).

questions.specification:
Poland has experienced steady economic growth in recent years, supported by a diverse industrial base, significant agricultural production, and growing service sectors. The country is a major producer of apples, exporting large quantities to neighboring European countries. The government imposes several taxes, including value-added tax (VAT) and progressive personal income tax. In 2022, policymakers considered pursuing expansionary monetary policies to stimulate the economy amid signs of a possible slowdown.

Table 1 shows selected macroeconomic data for Poland from 2019 to 2022.

| Year | Nominal GDP (PLN billions) | Price Index (2019 = 100) |
|------|---------------------------:|--------------------------:|
| 2019 |                      2300 |                       100 |
| 2020 |                      2400 |                       102 |
| 2021 |                      2510 |                       105 |
| 2022 |                      2640 |                       108 |

Table 2 outlines Poland’s major tax rates.

| Type of Tax      | Rate of Tax                    |
|------------------|--------------------------------|
| Corporate income tax (CIT) | 19 %                           |
| Personal income tax (PIT)  | Progressive, from 17 % to 32 % |
| Value-added tax (VAT)      | 23 %, 8 %, 5 %, 0 %            |

Poland’s Gini coefficient stands at 0.305, reflecting moderate income inequality. Estimates suggest that the average marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Poland is approximately 0.75. Table 3 provides recent data about the Polish apple market.

| Price (PLN per kg) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) |
|--------------------|---------------------------:|
| 3.00               |                    200,000 |
| 3.30               |                    170,000 |

In 2021, the total export revenue from Polish apples stood at 500 million PLN. By 2022, this figure had risen to 600 million PLN. These trends highlight Poland’s continued importance in the international apple market.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the rate of real GDP growth from 2021 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP in 2021 = 2510 ÷ (105/100) = 2390.48 (PLN billions, approximate).  
- Real GDP in 2022 = 2640 ÷ (108/100) = 2444.44 (PLN billions, approximate).  
- Rate of real GDP growth = [(2444.44 – 2390.48) ÷ 2390.48] × 100 ≈ 2.26%.

parts.content: If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Poland is 0.75, calculate the Keynesian multiplier for the Polish economy.
parts.markscheme:
- Formula for the Keynesian multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC).  
- Substitution: 1 ÷ (1 – 0.75) = 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate the price elasticity of demand for Polish apples as the price increases from 3.00 PLN per kg to 3.30 PLN per kg.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(170,000 – 200,000) ÷ 200,000] × 100 = –15%.  
- Percentage change in price = [(3.30 – 3.00) ÷ 3.00] × 100 = 10%.  
- Price elasticity of demand = (–15%) ÷ (10%) = –1.5.

parts.content: Using the information provided, calculate the change in the value of Poland’s apple export revenue from 2021 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:

- 2022 export revenue = 600 million PLN.  
- Change = 600 million PLN – 500 million PLN = 100 million PLN.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Polish government to help reduce income inequality in Poland.
parts.markscheme:
- The term “income inequality” refers to the uneven distribution of income among individuals in an economy, as indicated by Poland’s Gini coefficient of 0.305.  
- A recommended policy is to strengthen the existing progressive tax system, where higher-income earners face higher marginal tax rates, aligning with the current 17% to 32% brackets.  
- The Lorenz Curve diagram illustrates Poland’s income distribution, with the original curve showing moderate inequality (Gini = 0.305). A second, closer-to-equality curve shows the impact of stronger progressive taxation, narrowing the income gap by redistributing income toward lower earners. This supports higher overall consumption, reduces inequality, and strengthens demand in key sectors—making the policy both socially and economically effective.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aa253f77-da94-484f-b6ea-4a1dc8932005)
- An increase in the progressivity of personal income tax would raise additional government revenue from top earners, which could be redistributed through government spending on education, healthcare, or targeted subsidies for low-income groups.  
- The theoretical basis lies in reducing disposable income gaps (through direct taxation) and increasing economic equity, which can lead to higher aggregate consumption due to an MPC of 0.75.  
- This policy uses the concept that improving lower-income households’ spending power raises aggregate demand, creating potential positive effects on output and employment across the diverse industrial and service sectors.  
- The approach can be evaluated by noting that it may reduce incentives for top earners to work or invest if taxes are perceived as excessive, potentially slowing private investment.  
- Progressive taxation is nevertheless aligned with the goal of lowering income disparity, which can reduce social tensions and support a broader consumer base for Poland’s agricultural exports, including apples.  
- The unique conditions in Poland, such as moderate inequality levels and a significant share of agricultural production, suggest that more equitable income distribution can strengthen domestic consumption and maintain steady economic growth.  
- The policy might face administrative hurdles if tax avoidance increases, but effective enforcement and transparent public spending can address those concerns.  
- Overall, a more progressive tax structure promotes equity, supports domestic consumer demand, and uses existing government frameworks to achieve a decline in income inequality.

questions.specification:
**Trade Dynamics between Aridonia and Floristan**

Recent data from the Global Trade Council (GTC) highlights the shifting economic relationship between two neighboring nations, Aridonia and Floristan. Aridonia, a technologically advanced economy, has specialized heavily in the production of high-end Electronic Chips. Its manufacturing sector benefits from significant research and development (R&D) and a highly skilled workforce. Conversely, Floristan is characterized by its vast agricultural resources and a large labor force, making it highly efficient in the production of Textiles.

The GTC report suggests that through international trade, both nations have moved toward a more efficient allocation of global resources. Aridonia’s firms have been able to access larger international markets, allowing them to lower their average costs of production through mass manufacturing. Meanwhile, Floristan’s consumers have gained access to affordable high-tech goods that were previously too expensive to produce domestically.

However, the transition has not been without challenges. In Floristan, some domestic chip manufacturers have struggled to stay afloat as cheap imports from Aridonia flood the market. Critics argue that over-reliance on Textile exports may limit Floristan's long-term economic development by trapping it in a low-value-added sector. Proponents of free trade, however, point to the rapid transfer of technology and capital goods from Aridonia to Floristan, which they claim is essential for modernizing Floristan’s infrastructure and boosting its long-run growth potential.

Table 1 shows the maximum possible output of Electronic Chips and Textiles for each country if all resources are devoted to a single good. Table 2 illustrates the consumption levels of both nations after they have specialized according to their comparative advantage and engaged in trade.

**Table 1: Maximum Production (Units per worker/hour)**

| Country | Electronic Chips | Textiles |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aridonia | 20 | 10 |
| Floristan | 5 | 15 |

parts.content: Define the term *comparative advantage*.
parts.markscheme:
The ability of a country to produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost <marks>1 mark</marks> 

than another country. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two benefits of international trade for consumers in a country like Floristan.
parts.markscheme:
- Access to a wider variety of goods and services (increased choice). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower prices for goods that are produced more efficiently abroad. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher quality of goods due to increased international competition. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award Maximum 2 Marks</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the opportunity cost for Floristan to produce one unit of Electronic Chips.
parts.markscheme:
Opportunity cost is the amount of one good sacrificed to produce an additional unit of another.
- In Floristan, the resources used to produce 5 Chips could instead produce 15 Textiles.
- Opportunity cost of 1 Chip = $15 / 5$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Opportunity cost = 3 units of Textiles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a production possibility frontier (PPF) diagram for Aridonia, draw the impact of specialization and trade on Aridonia’s consumption possibilities.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/5e95ee6b-7f20-456b-84a0-e37cc52400e5.jpeg)

- Axes correctly labeled (Electronic Chips and Textiles) and a linear PPF drawn from 20 (Chips) to 10 (Textiles). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A 'Trade Line' or 'Consumption Possibility Curve' shown starting at 20 (Chips) but extending beyond the original PPF (e.g., to a point showing more textiles can be consumed). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation that trade allows the country to consume at a point outside its domestic PPF. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a domestic demand and supply diagram for Electronic Chips in Floristan, explain the impact of opening to trade on consumer surplus.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/a6d93717-e250-4711-bab1-383bb7aa1684.jpeg)

- Initially, Floristan is in equilibrium at $P_d$ (domestic price) and $Q_d$ (domestic quantity).
- When trade opens, Floristan can import Chips at the lower world price $P_w$.
- At $P_w$, domestic quantity demanded increases to $Q_2$, while domestic quantity supplied falls to $Q_1$.
- The fall in price from $P_d$ to $P_w$ leads to an increase in consumer surplus, represented by the area between $P_d$, $P_w$, and the demand curve.

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 2 and information from the text, explain how both Aridonia and Floristan achieve gains from trade through specialization.
parts.markscheme:
- Based on Table 1, Aridonia has a comparative advantage in Chips (OC = 0.5 Textiles) while Floristan has it in Textiles (OC = 0.33 Chips). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If both countries split resources 50/50 without trade, Aridonia would consume 10 Chips and 5 Textiles, while Floristan would consume 2.5 Chips and 7.5 Textiles.
- Looking at Table 2, after trade, Aridonia consumes 14 Chips and 6 Textiles, and Floristan consumes 6 Chips and 9 Textiles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both countries are consuming more of both goods than they could produce domestically in a balanced self-sufficiency model. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This represents a gain in welfare and material living standards due to the more efficient allocation of labor according to comparative advantage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a Long-Run Average Cost (LRAC) diagram, explain how international trade can lead to lower prices for consumers.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/94ca4909-f983-4337-b36d-0d64e0fade97.jpeg)

- Initially, a firm produces for a small domestic market at point $A$ with high average costs $AC_A$.
- International trade opens up larger global markets, allowing the firm to increase its scale of production to point $B$.
- As output increases from $Q_A$ to $Q_B$, the firm benefits from economies of scale (e.g., bulk buying, technical efficiencies), causing average costs to fall to $AC_B$.
- These lower production costs can be passed on to consumers in the form of lower prices.

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD-AS diagram, explain the possible impact of technology transfer from Aridonia on Floristan’s economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-18e32abc-ccdc-4bb8-90df-471fd73fd0d9-1771937587718/619c04cd-a272-4481-b7b4-83e2b3621bf6.jpeg)

- Technology transfer (e.g., importing advanced machinery or software) increases the quality and productivity of the factors of production in Floristan. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This increases the productive capacity of the economy, represented by a rightward shift of the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve from $LRAS_1$ to $LRAS_2$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This shift leads to an increase in potential output (Real GDP) from $Y_1$ to $Y_2$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- An increase in real output over time constitutes economic growth. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>Award 2 Marks for Diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>Award 4 Marks for Diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effects of specialization based on comparative advantage on the economic development of a country like Floristan.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**  
- **Specialization**: When a nation focuses its productive efforts on a limited range of goods where it has a comparative advantage.  
- **Economic development**: A multidimensional process involving improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, and structural changes in the economy.  

### **Arguments in Favor (Gains from Trade)**  
- **Increased Real Incomes**: As shown in Table 2, Floristan consumes more after trade (6 Chips, 9 Textiles) than it could produce alone. This increases material living standards.  
- **Resource Allocation**: By focusing on Textiles, Floristan uses its large labor force and land efficiently, generating export revenue to buy capital goods.  
- **Technology Transfer**: The text mentions the transfer of technology and capital goods from Aridonia. This can modernize Floristan’s industries and lead to higher productivity in other sectors (LRAS shift).  
- **Lower Prices**: Domestic consumers benefit from cheaper imports (e.g., Electronic Chips), increasing their disposable income for other developmental needs like education or health.

### **Arguments Against (Risks and Limitations)**  
- **Over-specialization**: Relying heavily on one sector (Textiles) makes Floristan vulnerable to changes in global demand or price fluctuations. If global textile prices fall, its export earnings will crash.  
- **Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis**: Primary products and low-value-added manufacturing (like textiles) often face declining terms of trade relative to high-tech goods (like chips) over time, potentially widening the gap between Aridonia and Floristan.  
- **Infant Industry Argument**: The text notes that domestic chip manufacturers in Floristan are struggling. Without protection, Floristan may never develop a high-tech sector, limiting its structural transformation.  
- **De-industrialization**: Job losses in the domestic manufacturing sectors that cannot compete with Aridonia could lead to regional unemployment and inequality.

### **Evaluation**  
- The benefits depend on the **nature of the good**. Textiles are low-value-added; if Floristan doesn't use the gains from trade to diversify, its development might stall.  
- **Government Policy**: The gains from trade only lead to development if the government invests export revenues into human capital (education) and infrastructure.  
- **Terms of Trade**: If the terms of trade favor Aridonia significantly, most of the gains from trade may be captured by the developed nation, leaving Floristan with minimal improvements in development.  
- **Conclusion**: While specialization provides immediate consumption gains (static efficiency), its long-term impact on development (dynamic efficiency) is uncertain and depends on whether it facilitates or hinders industrial diversification.

questions.specification:
## Economic Developments in Georgia

Georgia, a country situated in the South Caucasus region, has undergone considerable economic transformation since the early 2000s. With a population of around 3.7 million, its strategic location bridging Europe and Asia has encouraged policymakers to pursue an open economy model. Essential to this approach is the Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area (DCFTA) agreement with the European Union (EU). Over the last decade, Georgia’s real GDP growth averaged 4.5% annually. In 2022, real GDP growth reached 5.3%, partly driven by a rebound in tourism and rising demand for Georgian wine exports, which are major sources of foreign exchange.

Despite consistent growth, Georgia has faced issues of inflation. Following a surge in global energy and food prices, the National Bank of Georgia adopted a contractionary monetary policy stance in 2021, raising interest rates to curb price increases. These measures appeared to temper inflation, which fell from 13% in 2021 to 7.5% in 2022. However, local firms have complained that higher interest rates constrained their access to credit, slowing investment in crucial sectors like agriculture and small-scale manufacturing.

Agriculture, historically a cornerstone of the Georgian economy, is undergoing modernization through increased government support. The “Growth in Agriculture Initiative” introduced in 2020 provided subsidies for local farmers to invest in irrigation systems and advanced machinery. While the program expanded rural output, critics argue that these subsidies may disproportionately benefit large agribusinesses rather than smallholders. A notable microeconomic concern is the possibility of market distortion if subsidies create dependencies, discouraging resource allocation based on market signals.

Additionally, structural unemployment remains high at 11%. Many young people migrate from rural to urban areas, hoping to find jobs in Tbilisi’s growing service sector. In 2022, the official unemployment rate dropped slightly to 10.2%, but observers suggest that underemployment is prevalent, particularly in rural regions. The government has responded by expanding vocational education programs, with the aim of developing a workforce skilled in IT, construction, and tourism services.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows are essential for Georgia’s growth strategy. In 2019, net FDI stood at US 1.2 billion, but declined to US 0.9 billion in 2020 amid global economic uncertainties. Since 2021, improved investor sentiment and eased pandemic restrictions have begun to reverse this trend, with net FDI reaching US$1.1 billion by mid-2022. However, domestic entrepreneurs claim competition is skewed in favor of multinational corporations, which receive tax incentives the local firms cannot match. The government is reviewing its policies to ensure fair treatment of domestic producers while keeping Georgia attractive to foreign investors.

International trade patterns have also evolved. Apart from the EU, Georgia has trade ties with China, Turkey, and post-Soviet states. Wine, mineral water, and agricultural products account for a large share of Georgia’s exports. Despite new bilateral agreements aimed at lowering tariffs, non-tariff barriers such as stringent product standards still challenge Georgian exporters, particularly smaller producers who lack the finance to meet certification costs. Georgia’s currency, the lari (GEL), floated more freely in recent years, although the central bank maintains foreign reserves to smooth extreme exchange rate fluctuations.

The government’s current fiscal policy involves moderate levels of public spending, mainly directed toward infrastructure projects, including better road networks to facilitate trade. The budget deficit reached 4.8% of GDP in 2021, spurring debate over the sustainability of public debt, which stood at roughly 52% of GDP. Nonetheless, policymakers argue these infrastructure investments will foster long-term economic growth and encourage both domestic and foreign investments in the logistics sector.

Looking ahead, policymakers are focused on balancing economic expansion with social equity and external stability. The Gini coefficient has hovered around 0.37 in recent years, reflecting moderate income inequality. While tourism earnings, estimated at US$1.8 billion in 2022, buoyed Georgia’s external accounts, regional geopolitical tensions pose risks to both investor confidence and exports. In the coming years, the success of Georgia’s developmental agenda will hinge on its ability to foster inclusive growth, maintain price stability, and navigate the complexities of international trade.

**Table 1: Georgia’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                       | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|---------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)       | 17.5   | 15.9   | 16.8   | 18.2   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 5.0    | -6.2   | 4.8    | 5.3    |
| Inflation Rate (%)              | 4.9    | 5.2    | 13.0   | 7.5    |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)       | -2.4   | -8.7   | -5.1   | -4.8   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)           | 11.6   | 12.3   | 11.0   | 10.2   |
| Exchange Rate (GEL per US$)     | 2.84   | 3.06   | 3.15   | 2.90   |

**Table 2: Development & Trade Indicators for Georgia**

| Indicator                                      | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|------------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                      | 1.20   | 0.90   | 1.05   | 1.10   |
| Tourism Revenues (US$ billion)                 | 2.20   | 1.00   | 1.50   | 1.80   |
| Agriculture Subsidies (US$ million)            | 180    | 220    | 230    | 245    |
| Gini Coefficient                               | 0.38   | 0.37   | 0.37   | 0.37   |
| Exports of Goods (US$ billion)                 | 3.80   | 3.35   | 3.90   | 4.20   |
| Imports of Goods (US$ billion)                 | 8.60   | 7.90   | 8.20   | 8.60   |

parts.content: Define the term “monetary policy” referenced in paragraph (2).
parts.markscheme: Monetary policy refers to the adjustments of the interest rates and the availability of money in the economy set by the central bank.

parts.content: Define the term “subsidies” referenced in paragraph (3).
parts.markscheme: Monetary help (direct or indirect payment) offered by the government to firms (sometimes households) to aid in lowering costs of production.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the total increase in Georgia’s nominal GDP (in US$ billions) from 2020 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
Total increase in Georgia’s nominal GDP from 2020 to 2022  
- From Table 1, nominal GDP in 2020 = US$15.9 billion  
- From Table 1, nominal GDP in 2022 = US$18.2 billion  
- Total increase = 18.2 – 15.9 = US$2.3 billion

parts.content: Sketch an AD/AS diagram to illustrate the potential impact on inflation when real GDP growth outpaces growth in productive capacity, referring to the inflation data in Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
- If real GDP grows faster than productive capacity, the economy may approach or exceed its full employment level of output, leading to demand-pull inflation.  
- In an AD/AS diagram, AD shifts outward more quickly than AS, causing a higher price level.  
- Table 1 data shows inflation peaking at 13.0% in 2021 before moderating to 7.5% in 2022, suggesting that demand pressures contributed to inflation when the economy expanded strongly.

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply of labor diagram, explain how vocational education programs (paragraph 4) might help reduce structural unemployment in Georgia.
parts.markscheme:
- Vocational education improves workers’ skills, making their labor more adaptable to the changing structure of the economy (for example, Tbilisi’s growing service sector).  
- The supply of labor qualified for modern or emerging industries shifts right in a labor market diagram, decreasing the equilibrium level of unemployment.  
- Structural unemployment decreases because workers gain skills relevant to changing labor demand (e.g., IT, tourism).  
- As illustrated by the rightward shift of the supply curve of skilled labor, the economy fills industry-specific job vacancies more efficiently, reducing structural unemployment in the long run.

parts.content: Using a supply-and-demand of currency (foreign exchange) diagram, explain how improvements in tourism revenues (paragraph 8) could affect the value of the Georgian lari.
parts.markscheme:
- Rising tourism revenues increase foreign demand for the lari because tourists need local currency for goods and services.  
- In a currency market diagram, the demand curve for the lari shifts right, causing the lari to appreciate if supply remains constant.  
- An appreciation might strengthen external accounts but can also make exports relatively more expensive.  
- Tourism revenues of US$1.8 billion in 2022 (Table 2) signify a key foreign exchange earner that can bolster the lari’s value.

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how the sharp decline in real GDP growth in 2020 (Table 1) could have influenced cyclical unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
- The drop in real GDP growth from 5.0% in 2019 to -6.2% in 2020 (Table 1) suggests a recessionary phase in the business cycle.  
- During a recession, firms cut back production, decreasing the demand for labor and causing cyclical unemployment to rise.  
- In a business cycle diagram, this downturn would be represented by a movement below the potential output level.  
- By 2022, the recovery (with real GDP growth at 5.3%) likely helped reduce cyclical unemployment as economic conditions improved.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate Georgia’s development strategy of combining open trade policies, moderate subsidies, and infrastructure spending.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Development strategy: a set of policies to improve living standards, increase economic growth, and reduce poverty.  
- Open trade policies: policies that encourage free flow of goods and services internationally, lowering tariff and non-tariff barriers. 
-  Infrastructure spending: government investment in transport, energy, and communication networks to support economic activity.  
- Moderate Subsidies:  financial support provided by the government at a controlled or limited level to assist specific sectors or groups without causing significant market distortions or fiscal burden.

**Range of Arguments:**

Arguments in Favor of Georgia’s Development Strategy

- Open trade policies have bolstered export growth (e.g., wine and mineral water) and attracted FDI, contributing to an average real GDP growth of 4.5% in the last decade and an FDI rebound to US$1.1 billion by mid-2022 (Table 2).  
- The DCFTA with the EU expands market access for Georgia’s exports, potentially spurring higher export revenues and diversification of trade.  
- Moderate subsidies in agriculture can spur modernization, improve productivity (paragraph 3), and ensure food security. This is evidenced by rising agricultural output and increased subsidies from US 180 million to US 245 million (Table 2).  
- Infrastructure spending supports long-term growth through better transport networks, potentially attracting additional FDI and facilitating trade expansion. The budget deficit remains at 4.8% of GDP in 2022, suggesting manageable, if slightly elevated, government borrowing.

**Arguments Against or Limitations:**

- Open trade policies can expose local producers to intense foreign competition, and some domestic entrepreneurs feel competition is skewed in favor of multinational corporations (paragraph 5).  
- Agricultural subsidies may create market distortions, leading to inefficiency and resource misallocationif overproduction is encouraged and smaller farmers are marginalized.  
- Infrastructure spending raises concerns over public debt sustainability (about 52% of GDP). Higher future debt servicing could crowd out other essential government expenditure.  
- Despite strong growth, income inequality persists (Gini coefficient around 0.37), indicating that benefits are not evenly distributed. Rural underemployment remains, highlighting structural issues (paragraph 4).  
- External risks, such as regional geopolitical tensions, might disturb investor confidence, making reliance on open trade and FDI strategies uncertain.

**Evaluation and Conclusion:** 

- The strategy helps sustain economic growth, evident by robust real GDP increases and rising tourism revenues. These factors strengthen external accounts and draw in foreign capital.  
- Balancing social equity alongside open market reforms remains a challenge, as shown by persistent unemployment and inequality.  
- The success of this development model depends on continued infrastructure improvements, policies ensuring fair competition and inclusive benefits from subsidies, and structural reforms (e.g., enhanced vocational training).  
- In the long term, diversifying trade partners, maintaining macroeconomic stability, and effectively managing public debt are crucial to ensure sustained, inclusive growth, as they reduce external vulnerabilities and promote economic resilience.

questions.specification:
**Persistent current account deficits challenge the Republic of Zamara’s stability**

The central bank of Zamara has expressed alarm over the nation's deteriorating current account deficit, which widened to USD 14.80 billion in 2022/23 from USD 6.20 billion the previous fiscal year. Economic analysts attribute this to a combination of stagnant export revenues and a surge in the volume of imported goods. Because of the significantly lower prices of manufactured electronics and luxury vehicles in global markets compared to domestic alternatives, the government has proposed implementing strict import quotas on these non-essential items to preserve foreign exchange reserves.

Zamara's Finance Minister supported the move, stating that the reliance on external borrowing to fund consumption is unsustainable. However, he cautioned that roughly 40% of the country's imports are capital goods and industrial machinery, which are vital for the expansion of the agricultural and green energy sectors, particularly for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs).

To correct the trade imbalance, the Ministry of Planning has advocated for a managed depreciation of the Zar, the local currency. The Zar is currently estimated to be overvalued by approximately 25%. While depreciation could boost competitiveness, the Governor of the Central Bank warned that it might trigger cost-push inflation, particularly for fuel and food.

Despite these challenges, Zamara’s real GDP growth accelerated to 5.8% in 2023. To maintain this momentum, the government plans to double the share of total business loans allocated to SMEs from roughly 10% to 20% by 2025. Meanwhile, fiscal expansion has pushed public debt to 82% of GDP in 2023, well above the sustainability threshold of 70% recommended by international lenders.

Table 1: Zamara’s Current Account Data

| Year    | Exports (US$ billion) | Imports (US$ billion) | Current Account Deficit (US$ billion) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 2021/22 | 30.50                | 36.70                | 6.20                                |
| 2022/23 | 28.20                | 43.00                | 14.80                               |


Table 2: Key Economic Indicators

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage increase in Zamara’s current account deficit from 2021/22 to 2022/23.
parts.markscheme:
1. Percentage change formula and correct substitution of values:

$$\text{Percentage Increase} = \left(\frac{\text{New Deficit} - \text{Old Deficit}}{\text{Old Deficit}}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \left(\frac{14.80 - 6.20}{6.20}\right) \times 100$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

$$= \left(\frac{8.60}{6.20}\right) \times 100$$

$$= 138.71\%$$

parts.content: Draw a diagram to show the effect of a low price of foreign goods on the quantity imported by Zamara.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fce7008-8ed2-4057-b09b-62bfafbe05c2-1771840759346/9efe570b-5bfc-46f5-8a7b-c5beddaf97f3.jpeg)

- Correct supply and demand diagram for a domestic market. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- World price ($$P_w$$) shown below the domestic equilibrium price ($$P_d$$), with the gap between domestic quantity supplied ($$Q_{s1}$$) and domestic quantity demanded ($$Q_{d1}$$) correctly identified as imports. <marks>1 mark</marks>
